content.text: Non-violence is the supreme religion. All beings desire to live; none wish to die. Recognizing this, one who truly knows the Dhamma practices non-injury in thought, word, and deed toward all living beings.
Anekantavada — the doctrine of many-sidedness — holds that reality is complex and no single perspective can capture the full truth. From one perspective a thing exists; from another it does not.
Liberation (moksha) is attained through the Three Jewels: Samyak Darshana (right faith), Samyak Jnana (right knowledge), and Samyak Charitra (right conduct). All three must be cultivated together.

The soul (jiva) is distinct from non-soul (ajiva). Karma is fine matter that clings to the soul through attachment. Through right conduct, the influx of new karma is stopped and existing karma is shed.
The five great vows of Jain monastics are: Ahimsa (non-violence), Satya (truth), Asteya (non-stealing), Brahmacharya (celibacy), and Aparigraha (non-possession). These purify the soul.
Equanimity (samata) is the essence of Jain ethics. The wise one treats pleasure and pain, gain and loss, praise and blame with equal composure. This is the highest self-mastery.
